coaching.coaching: Plan how verification commands for documentation-update ACs will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: verification commands for documentation-update ACs

coaching.coaching: State the concrete attestation evidence the [operator-attested] actual line ranges of force-semantics docstrings will require (a change id / vote outcome / timestamp), recorded on the ticket.
coaching.subject: actual line ranges of force-semantics docstrings

coaching.coaching: Pin down two structurally separate force flags and their scopes with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: two structurally separate force flags and their scopes

coaching.coaching: Propagate the revision for unenumerated documentation surfaces defining force semantics to the child tickets.
coaching.subject: unenumerated documentation surfaces defining force semantics

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each documentation AC (e.g., `grep -rn 'ticket-system invariant' docs/ src/ ...` anchored to the required phrase) so that ACs 1–4 can be verified mechanically rather than by manual reading.
evidence: AC1: 'Public Python, CLI, MCP, user, and agent guidance consistently states that force bypasses all gates.' — no grep or doc-build command provided
AC2: 'Guidance explicitly classifies the open-children close prohibition as a ticket-system invariant rather than a gate.' — no verification command
AC3/4 similarly lack any concrete proving command
The Remediation section describes what to change but not how to verify the change is complete across all surfaces
findings.finding: ACs 1–4 are documentation-update claims with no proving command — no grep, doc-build check, or structured assertion is specified to verify that each surface has been updated with the required wording.
scenarios: An executor updates three of five surfaces and marks the ticket done because there is no mechanized check catching the omission
A reviewer cannot confirm completeness without manually reading every surface
findings.suggested_fix:
For each AC, add a grep command that anchors on a canonical phrase required to appear in each surface, e.g.:
```
# AC2 example:
grep -rn 'ticket-system invariant' docs/concurrency.md docs/force.md src/rebar/_lib_writes.py
# Must produce a match in every named file
```
Or add a doc-lint rule that asserts the phrase 'ticket-system invariant' (or equivalent) appears in each surface file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Evidence citation from `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:5-25` (module docstring + imports) to `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:498-518` (the `close_ticket()` body where the open-children guard is enforced unconditionally).
evidence: Plan states: 'src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:5-25 rejects closing a parent with open children regardless of force or force_close.'
Read probe of transition_close.py lines 5-25 shows only module docstring text and `from __future__ import annotations`, `import logging`, `import os`, `import subprocess` imports — no guard logic.

The actual unconditional enforcement is at lines 504-518: comment 'enforced UNCONDITIONALLY: neither --force ... nor --force-close ... can close a parent over open children' followed by the raise CommandError.
findings.finding: The plan's Evidence cites `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:5-25` as the location where closing a parent with open children is rejected regardless of `force` or `force_close`, but a read probe shows lines 5-25 are only the module docstring header and stdlib imports — no enforcement logic is present there. The actual unconditional guard is in the `close_ticket()` function at lines 498-518.
scenarios: An implementor checking whether the guard is truly unconditional is directed to lines 5-25 and finds no code, potentially questioning whether the plan's claim about the invariant is accurate.
A test author looking to understand what to contract-test against line 5-25 finds nothing actionable.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:5-25` with `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:498-518` in the Evidence section; the enforcement comment explicitly states 'neither --force (which bypasses the plan-review gate) nor --force-close ... can close a parent over open children.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Evidence citation from `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:20-24` (TYPE_CHECKING imports) to `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:329-366` (the `transition()` docstring at line 350-351, which incorrectly states `force` waives the unresolved-children guard).
evidence: Plan states: 'src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:20-24 describes force as waiving the unresolved-children guard.'
Read probe of _lib_writes.py lines 20-24 shows only: `from rebar.types import (ClaimResult, CreateResult, SignResult, TransitionResult, VerifySignatureResult,)` — no force semantics.
The actual force-waiving text is at _lib_writes.py lines 350-351: '``force`` also waives the unresolved-children guard when closing.'
findings.finding: The plan's Evidence cites `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:20-24` as the location where `force` is described as waiving the unresolved-children close restriction, but a read probe shows lines 20-24 are only TYPE_CHECKING import lines (ClaimResult, CreateResult, SignResult, TransitionResult, VerifySignatureResult). The actual contradicting docstring is at lines 350-351 (`'``force`` also waives the unresolved-children guard when closing.'`). Implementors directed to lines 20-24 will find no relevant text.
scenarios: An implementor following the plan's Evidence to line 20-24 of _lib_writes.py finds only import statements and cannot locate the docstring that contradicts the CLI help, causing them to miss the actual scope of the fix needed.
A test author trying to pin the 'wrong' behavior to a specific function signature/docstring is directed to the wrong part of the file.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:20-24` with `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:350-351` in the Evidence section; the relevant text is: '``force`` also waives the unresolved-children guard when closing.' inside the `transition()` function docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Evidence citation from `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:20-24` to `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:349-351` (the `transition()` docstring where `force` is incorrectly said to waive the open-children guard).
evidence: src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:20-24 contains only: 'from rebar.types import (ClaimResult, CreateResult, SignResult, TransitionResult, VerifySignatureResult)'
src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:349-351: '``force=True`` (optionally with a ``reason`` recorded in the audit comment) to bypass it. ``force`` also waives the unresolved-children guard when closing.'
findings.finding: The plan cites `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:20-24` as the location describing `force` as waiving the unresolved-children guard, but lines 20-24 are a TYPE_CHECKING import block. The conflicting docstring ('`force` also waives the unresolved-children guard when closing') is at lines 349-351 of that file.
scenarios: An implementer reads the cited lines (20-24), finds only imports, and cannot locate the text the plan is referring to — delaying or misdirecting the edit.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Evidence bullet to: `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:349-351` describes `force` as waiving the unresolved-children guard.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the corrected documentation that there are two force flags with distinct gate scopes: `--force` bypasses the plan-review start-work gate (now and for future start-work gates), while `--force-close=<reason>` bypasses the completion-verification close gate — and that neither bypasses the ticket-system open-children invariant.
evidence: AC1 says: 'Public Python, CLI, MCP, user, and agent guidance consistently states that force bypasses all gates.'
src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:350-351: 'force also waives the unresolved-children guard when closing' — the false claim being corrected.
src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:43-45: '--force  Bypass the plan-review gate when starting work (open->in_progress)' — shows force is scoped to the plan-review gate only.
src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:185: 'if force_close: return None' — only force_close bypasses the completion-verification gate; force does not.
The plan's remediation section says 'force bypasses all gates, including gates added in the future' as a single unified statement, but the two flags have distinct scopes.
findings.finding: The plan's AC1 says guidance should state 'force bypasses all gates', but the codebase has two structurally separate force flags that each bypass a different gate: `--force` bypasses only the plan-review start-work gate, while `--force-close` bypasses only the completion-verification close gate. Replacing the incorrect 'force waives the unresolved-children guard' text with 'force bypasses all gates' — without mapping each flag to its specific gate — risks replacing one inaccuracy with another: an agent or library caller reading 'force bypasses all gates' may incorrectly expect `--force` (alone) to bypass the completion-verification gate or future gates it currently has no effect on.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, line 1 ('force bypasses all gates'); src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:347-351; src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:43-45
scenarios: A library caller reads the updated docs, sees 'force bypasses all gates', passes `force=True` on a close expecting to skip the completion-verification gate, but the verifier still runs and blocks the close — the doc is still wrong after the fix.
An agent reads the corrected agent-guidance and attempts to close a ticket with `--force` instead of `--force-close`, expecting the completion gate to be bypassed.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC1 with: 'Guidance distinguishes `--force` (bypasses all start-work gates, including future ones) from `--force-close=<reason>` (bypasses all close-quality gates, including future ones); neither flag bypasses ticket-system invariants such as the open-children prohibition.' Update all corrected surfaces to use this two-flag framing.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise AC5 to reference the existing `test_transition_open_children` and `test_plan_review_gate` tests as the baseline coverage, and specify what NEW test surface is actually needed (e.g., a parameterized harness that runs with each registered gate, so a future gate addition automatically inherits the bypass/invariant contract).
evidence: tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_transition_open_children.py:51-63 — test_force_does_NOT_close_parent_with_open_child already asserts rc==1 and 'cannot be bypassed' in out when --force is passed
tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_transition_open_children.py:66-77 — test_force_close_does_NOT_close_parent_with_open_child already asserts the same for --force-close
tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_gate.py:165-169 — test_force_bypasses_gate_with_audit already asserts force=True bypasses the plan-review gate with an audit comment
tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_gate.py:201-207 — test_transition_to_in_progress_force_bypasses_with_audit already asserts the same for transition
AC5 text: 'Contract tests demonstrate that force bypasses enabled gates but cannot close a parent while any child remains non-closed' — no differentiation from existing coverage
findings.finding: AC5 calls for contract tests that demonstrate 'force bypasses enabled gates but cannot close a parent while any child remains non-closed,' but both halves of this behavioral contract are already covered by existing tests: `test_transition_open_children.py::test_force_does_NOT_close_parent_with_open_child` + `test_force_close_does_NOT_close_parent_with_open_child` (invariant half), and `test_plan_review_gate.py::test_force_bypasses_gate_with_audit` + `test_transition_to_in_progress_force_bypasses_with_audit` (gate-bypass half). Writing new contract tests from scratch would rebuild existing coverage (NIH), while the true gap — ensuring any NEW gate added in future is also covered — is not addressed by either the existing or the proposed tests.
scenarios: An implementer following AC5 literally duplicates the four existing tests under a new name, adding noise without closing a real gap.
The real untested invariant — that any gate added in the future is also bypassable by force — goes unaddressed because AC5's wording is satisfied by re-exercising today's plan-review gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC5 with: 'Existing tests `test_force_does_NOT_close_parent_with_open_child`, `test_force_close_does_NOT_close_parent_with_open_child`, `test_force_bypasses_gate_with_audit`, and `test_transition_to_in_progress_force_bypasses_with_audit` are confirmed as the behavioral contract baseline. A new parameterized test (or docstring update on the existing suite) enumerates all registered gates and asserts each is bypassable by `force`/`force_close` while the open-children invariant is not, so adding a gate automatically covers it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a testing deliverable to the Remediation section (or a dedicated 'Testing' subsection) that explicitly describes writing contract tests verifying: (a) `force` bypasses enabled gates, and (b) `force` cannot close a parent ticket with any non-closed child.
evidence: Remediation: 'Document the stable abstraction rather than enumerating today's gates' — entirely documentation-focused
Cascade flag: 'update all surfaces that define force semantics' — no mention of test surfaces
AC 5: 'Contract tests demonstrate that force bypasses enabled gates but cannot close a parent while any child remains non-closed' — test deliverable absent from Remediation
findings.finding: AC 5 requires contract tests ('Contract tests demonstrate that force bypasses enabled gates but cannot close a parent while any child remains non-closed'), but the Remediation section describes only documentation updates across surfaces — it contains no mention of writing or updating tests. An implementer following the Remediation would produce no tests, leaving AC 5 unverifiable.
scenarios: An implementer completes all documentation updates per the Remediation but writes no contract tests, satisfying the Remediation fully while failing AC 5.
A reviewer approving documentation-only PRs has no guidance that test files are in scope.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to Remediation: 'Add contract tests covering two invariants: (1) with force=True, a transition succeeds when blocked only by a gate; (2) with force=True (or force_close=True), attempting to close a parent ticket with at least one non-closed child raises an error. These tests should be parameterized over any new gates added in the future to prevent regression.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/plan-review-gate.md` to the enumerated edit targets in the Remediation section, since it explicitly documents `--force` gate-bypass semantics and must be updated consistently with the other surfaces.
evidence: docs/plan-review-gate.md:51-52: '`--force="<reason>"` bypasses it (audit-logged; on the `transition` path pass `--force` and the `--reason` text becomes the audit note)'
docs/plan-review-gate.md:440-443: '`--force` (CLI `rebar review-plan --force`, library/MCP `force=True`) bypasses **both**'
ABSENCE: docs/plan-review-gate.md does not appear in the plan's Evidence section or its Remediation cascade list
findings.finding: docs/plan-review-gate.md is a significant unenumerated consumer: it explicitly documents --force semantics at lines 51-52 ('`--force="<reason>"` bypasses it') and lines 440-443 ('`--force` ... bypasses **both**'), and defines the gate-bypass contract that the plan is clarifying. It is absent from both the Evidence section and the Remediation's enumerated update targets.
scenarios: Author updates all listed surfaces but leaves plan-review-gate.md with its existing gate-enumeration wording, creating a new inconsistency at the document that is most authoritative on gate semantics.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Remediation cascade list, add: `docs/plan-review-gate.md` (the gate-bypass definition at lines 51-52 and lines 440-443 must reflect the gates-not-invariants framing).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the target file/directory and test framework for the contract tests in AC5 (e.g., 'add to `tests/contract/test_force_semantics.py` using pytest') so the executor can proceed without investigation.
evidence: 'Contract tests demonstrate that force bypasses enabled gates but cannot close a parent while any child remains non-closed.' — no file path, no framework (pytest, unittest, etc.), no indication of new vs. existing test module
Remediation section does not mention a test directory or test file
findings.finding: AC5 references 'contract tests' with no specification of their location, test framework, or whether they are new or pre-existing, leaving the executor unable to determine where to write them without stopping to investigate.
scenarios: An executor is unsure whether to create a new tests/contract/ directory, extend an existing tests/test_transition.py, or add to integration tests — the choice affects scope and review surface
If the tests are expected in a specific location dictated by repo convention, the plan should state it; if not, the executor must guess
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC5 to: 'Contract tests in `tests/contract/test_force_semantics.py` (pytest) demonstrate that `force=True` bypasses each enabled gate and that `transition_close` raises `ChildTicketsOpenError` for a parent with non-closed children regardless of `force` value.' This makes the location, framework, and behavioral assertion concrete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate one canonical document (e.g., an ADR or an AGENTS.md section) as the authoritative definition of 'gate vs. ticket-system invariant' and have all other surfaces reference or derive from it, so the taxonomy has a single source of truth.
evidence: 'Document the stable abstraction rather than enumerating today's gates' — the plan directs this but does not specify where the canonical definition lives
AC items 1-4 require consistent wording across all surfaces but name no authoritative reference document
T5e check (c): 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc update' — absent from plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces a formal 'gate vs. ticket-system invariant' taxonomy as a cross-cutting architectural contract but designates no canonical authoritative location (ADR, AGENTS.md entry, or design doc) for this taxonomy; without a single source of truth, future contributors may re-introduce the inconsistency the plan is trying to fix.
scenarios: A new contributor adds a doc surface and is unsure what counts as a gate vs. an invariant, with no ADR to consult
A future gate is added and a developer wonders whether it is bypassable by force — no canonical reference exists
findings.suggested_fix: Add an ADR (e.g., docs/adr/NNN-force-semantics.md) that formally defines: (1) what a gate is, (2) what a ticket-system invariant is, (3) that force bypasses all gates including future ones, and (4) that force never bypasses invariants. Reference this ADR from every updated surface (Python docstring, CLI help, MCP schema, user guide, agent guidance).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC 1 (or add a dedicated AC) to enumerate the specific files/sections for each surface (e.g., `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py` docstring, `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py` help text, MCP tool-description field, AGENTS.md, user-guide page) so completeness is verifiable during review.
evidence: 'Cascade flag: public API contract wording affects library, CLI, MCP, user guide, and agent-facing guidance; update all surfaces that define force semantics' — no per-surface file targets given
AC 1 says 'Public Python, CLI, MCP, user, and agent guidance consistently states...' but names no files
docs/concurrency.md is cited in Evidence but no equivalent specificity is given for CLI/MCP/agent targets
findings.finding: The cascade flag states that all surfaces defining force semantics must be updated, but the ACs name only the abstraction-level requirements and do not enumerate the specific files or sections that need updating for CLI help text, MCP schema descriptions, and agent-facing guidance, leaving verification of completeness ambiguous.
scenarios: Reviewer approves the plan but MCP schema descriptions or CLI --help text are not updated because no AC pinned them
Agent-facing guidance lives in AGENTS.md or a prompt template that is not obviously covered by 'docs/' updates

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify the existing test file(s) where the AC5 contract tests should be added (e.g. the test module covering `transition_close.py` or `_lib_writes.py`) and name them explicitly in the Remediation section.
evidence: AC5: 'Contract tests demonstrate that force bypasses enabled gates but cannot close a parent while any child remains non-closed.'
ABSENCE: no test file path or existing test module is named in the plan's Remediation or Evidence sections to house these tests
findings.finding: AC5 requires new 'contract tests' ('demonstrate that force bypasses enabled gates but cannot close a parent while any child remains non-closed'), but the plan names no test file, test module, or directory where these tests should live — making this a creation item without a specified edit target.
scenarios: The implementer must decide where to add tests (new file vs. existing test module) without guidance, risking divergence from the project's test layout conventions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale explaining why a documentation-and-tests fix is the right scope for this change (e.g. the code already enforces the invariant correctly and unconditionally, so no structural change is needed — the tests in AC5 provide the regression guard against future drift).
evidence: Plan remediation: 'Document the stable abstraction rather than enumerating today's gates' — states rationale for the doc style, not for the doc-only approach.

AC5: 'Contract tests demonstrate that force bypasses enabled gates but cannot close a parent while any child remains non-closed.' — tests exist but no rationale for why tests are sufficient vs. structural enforcement.
ABSENCE: the plan contains no sentence explaining why the fix is documentation + tests rather than also restructuring the code to make the invariant more obviously unconditional.
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for the chosen scope (documentation-only fix) versus also adding a code-level assertion or guard that would fail loudly if the open-children check were ever accidentally gated behind a `force` flag in the future. The AC includes contract tests (AC5), but the plan does not explain why a test-only safety net is preferred over a structural enforcement (e.g. a dedicated `_check_open_children_invariant()` call placed before any force-bypass logic, making the invariant's unconditional nature architecturally self-evident).
scenarios: A future implementer refactors transition_close.py and mistakenly places the open-children check inside a branch that checks force_close, breaking the invariant silently until a contract test catches it.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
